examples.excerpt: After the introduction of transformer models in 2017, both patent and scientific publications increased greatly, with scientific publications exploding after the 2022 release of ChatGPT.
Since 2017, China has published more patent families in this field each year than all other countries combined.
However, since 2017 we can see that the GenAI share of all AI patents has been increasing (from 4.2% in 2017 to 6.1% in 2023) (Figure 11).

examples.excerpt: After the introduction of transformer models in 2017, both patent and scientific publications increased greatly, with scientific publications exploding after the 2022 release of ChatGPT.
The share of GenAI patent publications among all AI patents has shown a slight uptick since 2017, from 4.2% to 6.1% in 2023, and will likely further increase in future.

examples.excerpt: Since the introduction of the transformer in 2017, the deep neural network architecture behind the Large Language Models that have become synonymous with GenAI, the number of GenAI patents has increased by over 800%.
The share of GenAI patent publications among all AI patents has shown a slight uptick since 2017, from 4.2% in 2017 to 6.1% in 2023.
It is likely that the release of very successful and popular GenAI models and tools in 2022 (ChatGPT, Stable Diffusion, LlaMA, etc.) has initiated a new wave of GenAI research.

examples.excerpt: Patent activity has increased quite steadily since 2012 (compound annual growth rate, CAGR 2014–2019 = +8 percent) (Figure 7), indicating an increasing interest in the topic.

Given the findings in this patent landscape, we can expect the chloride process to progressively decrease within the next few years and an increasing use of direct hydrometallurgical processes, which require fewer steps and are more convenient.
The one emerging technology seems to be molten salt electrolysis developed by Pangang.

raw_output: {"excerpt":"An analysis based on filing jurisdictions of GenAI patent families provides an additional perspective. Members of patent families can be filed directly in one or more countries, via national patent offices, via the Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) route administered by WIPO or via the European Patent Convention route (EP) administered by the European Patent Office. Figure 20 shows that China is not only the leading inventor location in terms of GenAI patent families, but also the top country in terms of patent filings. Between 2014 and 2023, more than 40,000 GenAI patent families were filed in China for seeking patent protection. In the US, the number of patent families filed in the country reached more than 10,700 over the last decade. It is also worth noting that the Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) and the European Patent Convention (EP) are relevant routes for GenAI inventors to seek patent protection. Over the last decade, there have been more than 7,700 patent filings under the Patent Cooperation Treaty and more than 3,100 patent filings under the European Patent Convention."}

parsed_output.excerpt: An analysis based on filing jurisdictions of GenAI patent families provides an additional perspective. Members of patent families can be filed directly in one or more countries, via national patent offices, via the Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) route administered by WIPO or via the European Patent Convention route (EP) administered by the European Patent Office. Figure 20 shows that China is not only the leading inventor location in terms of GenAI patent families, but also the top country in terms of patent filings. Between 2014 and 2023, more than 40,000 GenAI patent families were filed in China for seeking patent protection. In the US, the number of patent families filed in the country reached more than 10,700 over the last decade. It is also worth noting that the Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) and the European Patent Convention (EP) are relevant routes for GenAI inventors to seek patent protection. Over the last decade, there have been more than 7,700 patent filings under the Patent Cooperation Treaty and more than 3,100 patent filings under the European Patent Convention.

raw_output: {"M":"A mapping f:Year→Count where each entry is (year, distinct_<category>_patent_family_count) representing the number of distinct patent families for a specific technology/domain in that year.","T":"The text provides a natural‑language description of the trend (e.g., growth rate, surge, slowdown) of patent families over time, which corresponds to the yearly counts listed in the table."}

parsed_output.M: A mapping f:Year→Count where each entry is (year, distinct_<category>_patent_family_count) representing the number of distinct patent families for a specific technology/domain in that year.
parsed_output.T: The text provides a natural‑language description of the trend (e.g., growth rate, surge, slowdown) of patent families over time, which corresponds to the yearly counts listed in the table.

raw_output: {"M":"For a sequence of (year, count) data points, the year‑over‑year growth rate for year y is (count_y - count_{y-1}) / count_{y-1}, and the compound annual growth rate (CAGR) over a span from year y₀ to year yₙ is (count_{yₙ} / count_{y₀})^{1/(yₙ - y₀)} - 1.","T":"Each example links a narrative about the evolution of patent families in a specific domain to a compact CSV table that lists the distinct patent family count for each year, showing an overall increasing trend that can be quantified using the growth‑rate formulas."}

parsed_output.M: For a sequence of (year, count) data points, the year‑over‑year growth rate for year y is (count_y - count_{y-1}) / count_{y-1}, and the compound annual growth rate (CAGR) over a span from year y₀ to year yₙ is (count_{yₙ} / count_{y₀})^{1/(yₙ - y₀)} - 1.
parsed_output.T: Each example links a narrative about the evolution of patent families in a specific domain to a compact CSV table that lists the distinct patent family count for each year, showing an overall increasing trend that can be quantified using the growth‑rate formulas.

raw_output: {"M":"Let C(y) be the count of distinct patent families in year y (y∈ℤ). For each example, C(y) is a non‑negative integer satisfying C(y) ≥ C(y‑1) (monotonic non‑decreasing) and can be approximated by an exponential growth model C(y) ≈ a·b^{(y‑y₀)} with a>0, b>1 over the observed interval.","T":"Each table records the yearly number of distinct patent families for a specific technology domain, and across all examples the counts consistently rise over time, often accelerating in later years, reflecting a generally increasing (often exponential‑like) trend in patent activity."}

parsed_output.M: Let C(y) be the count of distinct patent families in year y (y∈ℤ). For each example, C(y) is a non‑negative integer satisfying C(y) ≥ C(y‑1) (monotonic non‑decreasing) and can be approximated by an exponential growth model C(y) ≈ a·b^{(y‑y₀)} with a>0, b>1 over the observed interval.
parsed_output.T: Each table records the yearly number of distinct patent families for a specific technology domain, and across all examples the counts consistently rise over time, often accelerating in later years, reflecting a generally increasing (often exponential‑like) trend in patent activity.

parsed_output.M: For each example, let C(y) be the distinct patent‑family count for year y. The narrative describes the behavior of the sequence {C(y)}: monotonic increase (C(y) > C(y‑1)), monotonic decrease (C(y) < C(y‑1)), a peak (y* = argmax_y C(y)), or a slowdown (change in sign of ΔC(y) = C(y)‑C(y‑1)). Growth metrics may be expressed as year‑over‑year percent change g(y) = (C(y)‑C(y‑1))/C(y‑1)·100% or as a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) over an interval [y0,y1]: CAGR = (C(y1)/C(y0))^(1/(y1‑y0))‑1.
parsed_output.T: The text summarises the trend observed in the yearly patent‑family counts, stating whether the counts are rising, falling, peaking, or slowing down, and may cite the magnitude of growth (e.g., a high CAGR) for the given technology or region.

raw_output: {"M":"Let C_y denote the distinct patent family count for year y. Define the year‑over‑year change Δ_y = C_y - C_{y-1} and the year‑over‑year growth rate r_y = Δ_y / C_{y-1} (for C_{y-1}>0). Identify the peak year y* = argmax_y C_y. A trend is described as increasing when r_y>0 over successive years and decreasing when r_y<0. The compound annual growth rate (CAGR) over a period from year y0 to y1 is given by (C_{y1}/C_{y0})^{1/(y1-y0)} - 1.","T":"Across the examples the text consistently reports how the number of distinct patent families for a given technology evolves over time, noting periods of rapid increase, slowdown, peaks, and declines, and quantifies these dynamics using year‑over‑year changes, growth rates, and compound annual growth rates."}

parsed_output.M: Let C_y denote the distinct patent family count for year y. Define the year‑over‑year change Δ_y = C_y - C_{y-1} and the year‑over‑year growth rate r_y = Δ_y / C_{y-1} (for C_{y-1}>0). Identify the peak year y* = argmax_y C_y. A trend is described as increasing when r_y>0 over successive years and decreasing when r_y<0. The compound annual growth rate (CAGR) over a period from year y0 to y1 is given by (C_{y1}/C_{y0})^{1/(y1-y0)} - 1.
parsed_output.T: Across the examples the text consistently reports how the number of distinct patent families for a given technology evolves over time, noting periods of rapid increase, slowdown, peaks, and declines, and quantifies these dynamics using year‑over‑year changes, growth rates, and compound annual growth rates.

raw_output: {"M":"Let Y be the set of years. Define a function C: Y → ℕ where C(y) gives the number of distinct patent families reported for year y in the specific category or region described in the accompanying text.","T":"The table provides, for each year, the count of distinct patent families for the technology, domain, or country mentioned in the text, showing how patent activity has changed over time."}

parsed_output.M: Let Y be the set of years. Define a function C: Y → ℕ where C(y) gives the number of distinct patent families reported for year y in the specific category or region described in the accompanying text.
parsed_output.T: The table provides, for each year, the count of distinct patent families for the technology, domain, or country mentioned in the text, showing how patent activity has changed over time.

parsed_output.M: Let Y be an ordered set of years. For each year y ∈ Y there is an associated integer n(y) representing the number of distinct patent families for a specific category (e.g., technology, region, or data modality). The sequence {n(y)} across Y exhibits an overall increasing trend over the decade, often approximating a compound annual growth rate (e.g., ~45% per year) after a certain inflection point, with possible local peaks, plateaus, or declines noted in the narrative.
parsed_output.T: Each example pairs a narrative describing the evolution of patent activity (e.g., rapid growth, slowdown, peak years, or sector‑specific dynamics) with a compact CSV table that lists, for each year, the count of distinct patent families in that category. The shared pattern is that the table quantifies the yearly patent family counts, and the text interprets these counts to highlight overall growth, notable surges, or recent declines.

examples.excerpt: Since 2000, China has experienced the highest patent growth rates of any of the top 15 inventor locations. China’s patent growth has slowed in recent years, leading to an annual growth rate of 14% between 2019 and 2024 (Figure 3.6).
India has emerged as the fastest-growing innovation location in vehicle efficiency, showing a surge in patent publications from 66 in 2019 to 300 in 2024. This corresponds to a robust 35% CAGR.
Sweden has achieved the highest patent growth rate in the area of green digitalization for heavy‑duty road transport, at a compound annual growth rate of 27% per annum (Figure 6.4).

raw_output: {"examples":[{"excerpt":"In terms of growth of patent families, image/video- and molecules/genes/proteins- based GenAI patent families increased the most over the last decade (an average annual growth rate of around 45% between 2014 and 2023)."},{"excerpt":"Not only does China have the largest share of all patent families published globally but also is growing at the rate of 50% per year. However, India has an even higher growth rate of 56% per year even though its world share is still small."},{"excerpt":"In contrast, diffusion models and LLMs show much higher growth rates over the last three years, with the number of patent families for diffusion models increasing from 18 in 2020 to 441 in 2023 and for LLMs increasing from 53 in 2020 to 881 in 2023."}]}

examples.excerpt: In terms of growth of patent families, image/video- and molecules/genes/proteins- based GenAI patent families increased the most over the last decade (an average annual growth rate of around 45% between 2014 and 2023).

Not only does China have the largest share of all patent families published globally but also is growing at the rate of 50% per year. However, India has an even higher growth rate of 56% per year even though its world share is still small.

In contrast, diffusion models and LLMs show much higher growth rates over the last three years, with the number of patent families for diffusion models increasing from 18 in 2020 to 441 in 2023 and for LLMs increasing from 53 in 2020 to 881 in 2023.

raw_output: {"excerpt":"In terms of patent growth, patent families assigned to GANs show the strongest increase over the past decade. However, patent growth of GAN patent families has slowed down and GAN patent families have increased only moderately over the last three years. A similar slowdown in patent growth can be seen for patent families for VAE models and autoregressive models. In contrast, diffusion models and LLMs show much higher growth rates over the last three years, with the number of patent families for diffusion models increasing from 18 in 2020 to 441 in 2023 and for LLMs increasing from 53 in 2020 to 881 in 2023."}

parsed_output.excerpt: In terms of patent growth, patent families assigned to GANs show the strongest increase over the past decade. However, patent growth of GAN patent families has slowed down and GAN patent families have increased only moderately over the last three years. A similar slowdown in patent growth can be seen for patent families for VAE models and autoregressive models. In contrast, diffusion models and LLMs show much higher growth rates over the last three years, with the number of patent families for diffusion models increasing from 18 in 2020 to 441 in 2023 and for LLMs increasing from 53 in 2020 to 881 in 2023.

raw_output: {"excerpt":"In terms of patent growth, patent families assigned to GANs show the strongest increase over the past decade. In contrast, diffusion models and LLMs show much higher growth rates over the last three years, with the number of patent families for diffusion models increasing from 18 in 2020 to 441 in 2023 and for LLMs increasing from 53 in 2020 to 881 in 2023. The GenAI boom caused by modern chatbots such as ChatGPT has clearly increased research interest in LLMs."}

parsed_output.excerpt: In terms of patent growth, patent families assigned to GANs show the strongest increase over the past decade. However, patent growth of GAN patent families has slowed down and GAN patent families have increased only moderately over the last three years. A similar slowdown in patent growth can be seen for patent families for VAE models and autoregressive models. In contrast, diffusion models and LLMs show much higher growth rates over the last three years, with the number of patent families for diffusion models increasing from 18 in 2020 to 441 in 2023 and for LLMs increasing from 53 in 2020 to 881 in 2023. The GenAI boom caused by modern chatbots such as ChatGPT has clearly increased research interest in LLMs.

parsed_output.excerpt: Figure 17a Country comparison of the number of GenAI-related patent families, 2014–2023 (Y‑axis). Not only does China have the largest share of all patent families published globally but also is growing at the rate of 50% per year. However, India has an even higher growth rate of 56% per year even though its world share is still small. The Republic of Korea has also achieved high growth rates in their GenAI patent families since 2014. By contrast, patent families from Japan and the UK have only risen by a little more than 10% per year on average.

raw_output: {"excerpt":"In terms of growth of patent families, image/video- and molecules/genes/proteins- based GenAI patent families increased the most over the last decade (an average annual growth rate of around 45% between 2014 and 2023). In contrast, the total number of GenAI patent families based on speech/sound/music data has increased only slightly since 2021 (Figure 24)."}

raw_output: {"excerpt":"In general, patent growth has been high in all applications since 2014. However, over the last three years there have been diverging growth trends with very high growth rates in both smaller application areas such as agriculture and energy management and large fields such as life sciences, security and physical sciences/engineering. In contrast, the number of patent families has stagnated or even declined in certain application areas such as telecommunications, military, arts and humanities or industrial property/law/social and behavioral sciences (Figure 27)."}

parsed_output.excerpt: In general, patent growth has been high in all applications since 2014. However, over the last three years there have been diverging growth trends with very high growth rates in both smaller application areas such as agriculture and energy management and large fields such as life sciences, security and physical sciences/engineering. In contrast, the number of patent families has stagnated or even declined in certain application areas such as telecommunications, military, arts and humanities or industrial property/law/social and behavioral sciences (Figure 27).

raw_output: {"excerpt":"Patents in the use of GenAI in energy management have seen the largest growth, both since 2018 and also in the last three years, with applications in agriculture also growing more in the last three years."}

parsed_output.excerpt: Patents in the use of GenAI in energy management have seen the largest growth, both since 2018 and also in the last three years, with applications in agriculture also growing more in the last three years.

raw_output: {"excerpt":"Titanium is a metal with high industrial and commercial interest. It is used in many applications in the form of dioxide, elementary metal or in alloys. Titanium is mainly extracted from ilmenite ore (82 percent), other sources being slags (13 percent) and rutile ore (5 percent). About 94 percent of the world’s titanium is used to produce titanium dioxide, with the remaining 6 percent being used for titanium metal and alloys."}

parsed_output.excerpt: Titanium is a metal with high industrial and commercial interest. It is used in many applications in the form of dioxide, elementary metal or in alloys. Titanium is mainly extracted from ilmenite ore (82 percent), other sources being slags (13 percent) and rutile ore (5 percent). About 94 percent of the world’s titanium is used to produce titanium dioxide, with the remaining 6 percent being used for titanium metal and alloys.
